us-politics.headline: U.S. Airman Rescued After Jets Downed Over Iran
us-politics.body:
U.S. military forces rescued an airman shot down over Iran, Trump confirmed, as the conflict enters a new and costly phase. The downed jets mark the first U.S. aircraft lost to enemy fire in over 20 years, a milestone that underscores the war's escalating intensity. Trump has described the campaign as near completion while simultaneously raising new threats against Tehran.

Analysts and commentators are divided on what the losses mean. Supporters frame the rescue as a demonstration of U.S. capability and resolve. Critics question whether the administration's public optimism matches the battlefield reality. The losses add pressure to an administration already managing a wartime footing alongside a packed domestic agenda.

sources.title: Trump says U.S. military has rescued airman shot down over Iran
Sunday shows preview: Trump ups threats on Iran after saying war ‘near completion’
TEVI TROY: Trump faces the burdens of a wartime presidency
US military jets hit in Iran war are the first shot down by enemy fire in over 20 years

us-politics.headline: Courts Check Trump on Colleges, White House Ballroom
us-politics.body:
A federal judge blocked the Trump administration's effort to require colleges to certify that race played no role in admissions decisions, dealing a setback to its post-affirmative-action enforcement push. The ruling limits a key tool the administration had sought to use against universities.

Separately, Trump filed an emergency appeal to continue construction of a ballroom inside the White House complex after a court moved to halt the project. The legal fight has drawn attention to questions about the use of public space for what critics call a private commercial venue. The administration argues the project is lawful and within presidential prerogative. Both cases illustrate an ongoing pattern of judicial friction with White House priorities.

sources.title: Judge blocks Trump bid to require colleges to prove race not considered in admissions
What we know about the White House ballroom bunker
Trump files emergency appeal to keep building White House ballroom

us-politics.headline: ICE Crash Dispute and FBI Warning Fuel Immigration Debate
us-politics.body:
The Department of Homeland Security pushed back hard against Democratic Senator Chris Van Hollen, asserting that an illegal immigrant caused a crash while fleeing ICE agents - contradicting Van Hollen's framing of the incident. DHS called his characterization misleading. The dispute reflects the sharp political battle over how immigration enforcement incidents are described and attributed.

Meanwhile, FBI Director Kash Patel issued a blunt public warning to anyone who attacks law enforcement, saying the bureau would 'put you down.' Supporters praised the statement as a necessary show of strength. Critics raised concerns about the tone and its implications for civil liberties. Together, the two episodes highlight how law enforcement rhetoric and immigration policy remain flashpoints between the parties.

sources.title: DHS slams Democrat Sen Chris Van Hollen claim, says illegal alien caused crash while fleeing ICE
FBI's Patel delivers blunt warning to law enforcement attackers: 'We're going to put you down'

us-politics.headline: Wisconsin Race Exposes GOP Cracks; Louisiana Parade Attack Injures Several
us-politics.body:
Wisconsin's state Supreme Court race is drawing national attention as a test of Republican strength in a key battleground. The Hill reports the state GOP is struggling to consolidate support, with the race spotlighting internal tensions that could complicate the party's broader electoral strategy heading into future cycles.

In Louisiana, a vehicle drove into a crowd at a local parade, injuring several people. Authorities are investigating the incident. Details on motive and the driver's identity remain limited. The event follows a series of vehicle-ramming incidents at public gatherings across the country in recent years, though officials have not yet indicated whether this was intentional.

sources.title: Several injured after vehicle drives into crowd at Louisiana parade
Wisconsin judicial race spotlights state GOP’s struggles

au-politics.headline: Australia scrambles to secure fuel supply amid price crackdown
au-politics.body:
Prime Minister Albanese is working to avert a fuel shortage after disruptions rattled supply chains, with Japan and other Asian nations now assuring Australia of normal supply continuity. The diplomatic push comes as pressure mounts domestically over price gouging at the bowser.

Nearly 100 NSW service stations have been fined $1,100 each for displaying misleading prices, and the state government is funding a tip-off scheme urging motorists to report suspicious hikes. Western Australia is expanding its FuelWatch scheme to cover all regional stations from May, requiring mandatory price reporting. Farmers are among the hardest hit, with some saying rising fuel costs make it difficult to cover basic living expenses.

sources.title: 'Struggling massively': Farmer finds it hard to put food on the table
Nearly 100 NSW service stations fined $1,100 each over misleading petrol prices amid fuel shortage crackdown
Asian countries assure Australia ‘normal supply’ of fuel will continue as Albanese focuses on averting shortages
Japan assures Australia will get 'normal supply' of fuel
All WA regional stations must report fuel prices from May as FuelWatch scheme expands
Motorists urged to dob in service stations over fuel price hikes

au-politics.headline: Melbourne murder suspect caught fleeing country at airport
au-politics.body:
A man was arrested at Melbourne Airport while attempting to board an international flight and has been charged with murdering a woman. Police did not release details on the victim or the accused's intended destination.

Separately, in Tasmania, a man remains in serious condition after being shot by police in Goodwood. The sisters of Victorian officer Neal Thompson, killed by Dezi Freeman, announced plans to sue Victoria Police, describing the process of seeking accountability as painful. The three incidents highlight a difficult weekend for law enforcement agencies across multiple states.

sources.title: Man arrested attempting to board international flight at Melbourne airport charged with murdering woman
Man in serious condition after being shot by Tasmania Police
'Painful': Sisters of officer slain by Dezi Freeman to sue Victoria Police

au-politics.headline: Victoria's First Nations treaty body holds historic first elections
au-politics.body:
Victoria's First Nations treaty body has begun its inaugural elections, a milestone in the state's formal treaty process. Participants described the moment as an assertion of self-determination, with one voice quoted saying communities are the experts in their own lives.

The elections mark a concrete step in a process that has moved slowly since Victoria became the first Australian state to legislate a treaty framework. The outcome will shape who negotiates on behalf of First Nations communities in formal talks with the state government. Progress on treaty remains uneven nationally, with no other state at a comparable stage.

sources.title: ‘We’re the experts in our own lives’: Victoria’s First Nations treaty body is holding its first elections

au-politics.headline: Australians mark Easter amid global uncertainty and local disruptions
au-politics.body:
Australians celebrated Easter across the long weekend despite fuel cost pressures, with thousands attending the National Folk Festival in Canberra and communities gathering for traditional events. A major highway reopened after Cyclone Narelle, allowing Easter road trips to resume in affected regions.

A magnitude-5.5 earthquake rattled a remote APY Lands community early on Sunday morning, described by locals as a sharp wake-up call though no serious injuries were reported. Australian flotilla activists are preparing to return to Gaza as the wider conflict continues, with at least one asking publicly whether they will face punishment for going back. The weekend also brought a boxing title for Tim Tszyu, who dominated Denis Nurja in Wollongong.

sources.title: Tim Tszyu dominates Denis Nurja to claim title in Wollongong
Australians find joy amid global uncertainty in Easter celebrations
Travellers celebrate as major highway reopens after Cyclone Narelle
Magnitude-5.5 earthquake 'morning wake-up call' to remote community
Thousands flock to National Folk Festival despite fuel cost concerns
‘Will I be punished for daring to return?’ Australian flotilla activists to head back to Gaza as wider war rages

world.headline: US-Iran War Escalates as Pilot Rescued, Infrastructure Targeted
world.body:
The US-Israeli military campaign against Iran intensified over the past 24 hours, with strikes hitting universities, a Tehran psychiatric hospital, and other civilian infrastructure. Trump confirmed the rescue of a downed US pilot, saying simply "we got him" - the officer had been shot down over Iranian territory in an incident now drawing intense scrutiny. Iran fired on a Kuwait oil complex and rejected a US ultimatum, while continuing to assert control over the Strait of Hormuz, though it signalled Iraqi ships may pass freely as regional shipping patterns shift.

US satellite firm Planet Labs announced a blackout on imagery of the conflict zone, raising press freedom concerns. Public opinion polls show the war is deeply unpopular domestically. Pakistan is navigating serious economic and diplomatic fallout from the conflict on its doorstep.

sources.title: Trump’s unpopular war on Iran
Iran says Iraqi ships can pass Strait of Hormuz as transits tick up
US satellite firm Planet Labs announces blackout on war on Iran images
Iran war live: Trump confirms rescue of US officer, says ‘we got him’
US says it has arrested relatives of late Iranian ​general Qasem ​Soleimani
How will Pakistan deal with the fallout from Iran war?
What we know so far about the US fighter jet shot down over Iran
Tehran psychiatric hospital not usable after US-Israeli strike
Universities hit as US, Israel ramp up attacks on Iran’s infrastructure
Iran’s coastline: architecture of control

world.headline: Russia Kills Five in Ukraine Market Strike
world.body:
A Russian attack on a Ukrainian market killed five people, the latest in a pattern of strikes targeting civilian gathering points. The attack drew renewed condemnation but no immediate shift in the military situation on the ground.

Separately, Estonia pushed back against circulating rumours that the Russian-speaking city of Narva sought to join Russia as a "People's Republic." Estonian officials dismissed the claims as disinformation, though the episode highlights ongoing Russian influence operations targeting Baltic states with significant Russian-speaking minorities.

sources.title: Russian attack on Ukraine market kills five
'People's Republic' rumors: Does an Estonian city really want to join Russia?

world.headline: Germany Eyes Military Travel Curbs for Young Men
world.body:
Germany is considering requiring men under 45 to obtain military approval before taking extended stays abroad, a significant step reflecting Berlin's push to rebuild defence readiness amid the broader European security crisis. The proposal has not yet passed into law but signals how seriously German planners are treating potential mobilisation scenarios.

In a related sign of shifting European defence postures, Indonesia received the bodies of UN peacekeepers killed in southern Lebanon, underlining the human cost of ongoing instability across multiple conflict zones.

sources.title: German males under 45 may need military approval for long stays abroad
Indonesia receives bodies of peacekeepers killed in southern Lebanon

world.headline: Israel Tightens Hebron Mosque Access, Arrests Tel Aviv Protesters
world.body:
Israeli authorities continued to restrict Palestinian access to the Ibrahimi Mosque in Hebron, with new measures further limiting entry for Muslim worshippers at one of Islam's holiest sites. Critics say the restrictions amount to a creeping annexation of shared religious space.

Inside Israel, police arrested antiwar demonstrators in Tel Aviv protesting the country's military campaigns. The arrests signal growing domestic tension over the government's simultaneous prosecution of conflicts in Gaza and Iran. Israeli drone strikes also continued in central Gaza.

sources.title: Israeli measures tighten grip on Hebron’s Ibrahimi Mosque
Israeli police arrest antiwar protesters in Tel Aviv
Vehicle engulfed in flames after Israeli drone strike in central Gaza

ai.headline: Claude Code Leak Spawns Malware, Pricing Backlash
ai.body:
Anthropic is dealing with two Claude Code headaches at once. Hackers are distributing leaked Claude Code files bundled with malware, targeting developers who download unofficial copies. The security risk is real and spreading fast.

Meanwhile, Anthropic announced that Claude Code subscribers will need to pay extra to use OpenClaw, a move that has frustrated users who expected the feature to be included. The pricing change lands at the worst possible moment, as the malware campaign erodes trust in the product's ecosystem. Anthropic has not yet detailed how it plans to address either problem.

sources.title: Anthropic says Claude Code subscribers will need to pay extra for OpenClaw usage
Hackers Are Posting the Claude Code Leak With Bonus Malware

ai.headline: AI Fakes and Copyright Trolls Are Targeting Small Creators
ai.body:
A folk musician named Murphy Campbell found himself caught between two modern threats: AI-generated fakes of his music and a copyright troll exploiting the confusion. The combination left him fighting legal and reputational battles he never anticipated.

His case reflects a broader pressure on independent creators. As AI-generated content floods platforms, bad actors use the chaos to file fraudulent copyright claims against the real artists. The burden of proof falls on the creator. That problem connects directly to a growing push for "human-made" certification labels, as artists and audiences alike scramble for ways to distinguish genuine work from synthetic output.

sources.title: A folk musician became a target for AI fakes and a copyright troll
Really, you made this without AI? Prove it

ai.headline: Tech Giants Move to Gut Colorado's Right-to-Repair Law
ai.body:
Colorado passed one of the strongest right-to-repair laws in the country, and tech companies are already working to hollow it out. Industry lobbyists are pushing amendments that would create broad exemptions, effectively shielding manufacturers from the law's core requirements.

The fight in Colorado is a test case for the national right-to-repair movement. If companies succeed in weakening the law through the legislative back door, it signals a playbook other states will face. Supporters of the original law say the proposed changes would make it nearly unenforceable.

sources.title: Tech companies are trying to neuter Colorado’s landmark right-to-repair law

btc.headline: Bitcoin Sentiment Sours, But History Favors the Patient
btc.body:
Bearish chatter about Bitcoin on social media has hit a five-week high, according to on-chain analytics firm Santiment. Price stagnation is driving the negativity, with retail sentiment souring as BTC struggles to find direction.

The timing may matter less than it seems. Research from Mercado Bitcoin finds that Bitcoin has consistently outperformed both gold and equities in the months following major global shocks. Periods of peak pessimism have historically preceded recoveries. The current mood, while uncomfortable, fits a familiar pattern for long-term holders who have watched sentiment cycles play out before.

sources.title: Bitcoin bearish social chatter reaches 5-week high: Santiment
Bitcoin tends to outperform gold and stocks after global shocks, Mercado Bitcoin finds

btc.headline: Bitcoin's Quantum Threat Is Real. The Fix Is Complicated.
btc.body:
With Bitcoin's market cap sitting at $1.3 trillion, the blockchain's vulnerability to future quantum computing attacks is drawing serious attention. CoinDesk outlines several active initiatives aimed at making Bitcoin's cryptographic foundations quantum-resistant, a challenge that requires broad developer consensus and protocol-level changes.

The core problem is that quantum computers, once powerful enough, could theoretically break the elliptic curve cryptography protecting Bitcoin wallets. No such machine exists today, but the window to act is narrowing. Upgrading Bitcoin requires navigating its notoriously slow governance process, making the timeline as much a political challenge as a technical one.

sources.title: Bitcoin's $1.3 trillion security race: Key initiatives aimed at quantum-proofing the world's largest blockchain

btc.headline: Iran Banned Telegram. It Didn't Work.
btc.body:
Telegram founder Pavel Durov says Iran's government ban on the messaging platform backfired, driving more users toward the app rather than away from it. Durov made the comments publicly, framing the move as a case study in how state censorship tends to amplify the reach of the platforms it targets.

The episode is relevant to the crypto space because Telegram remains a primary communication hub for crypto communities globally. Any sustained crackdown on the platform in major markets would carry real consequences for project coordination, token launches, and community organizing.

sources.title: Telegram founder Pavel Durov says Iranian government's ban backfired

food.name: Miso-Glazed Aubergine (Nasu Dengaku)
food.description: Halved aubergines broiled until silky and caramelised under a sweet white miso and mirin glaze
food.tip: Score the flesh in a crosshatch before glazing so the miso penetrates deeper and the edges char without burning

exercise.name: Sunday Staircase Pyramid
exercise.equipment: A staircase of at least 10 steps
exercise.description: Climb the stairs 1 flight, rest 15 seconds, then 2 flights, rest 20 seconds, building to 6 flights then descending back down the pyramid — pure cardio and leg strength with zero gym required

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus and Personal Identity Over Time
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had every component replaced remains fundamentally the same thing — philosophers like Derek Parfit extended this to human consciousness, asking whether the 'you' of today is truly the same person who existed ten years ago
learn.why: On a quiet Sunday it's a genuinely unsettling and productive rabbit hole: Parfit's conclusions in Reasons and Persons suggest personal identity is less fixed than we assume, which has surprising implications for how seriously you should hold grudges, fear death, or feel bound by past commitments
